coredata.dc:title: Low temperature synthesis of carbon dots in microfluidic chip and their application for sensing cefquinome residues in milk
coredata.dc:creator: Li, Guangming
Liu, Chen
Zhang, Xingcai
Zhai, Peng
Lai, Xinyi
Jiang, Wenxiao
coredata.dc:description: In this study, the N-doped carbon dots were continuously synthesized by a facile microfluidic strategy at 90 °C, and their quantum yields reached 19.2%. The characteristics of the obtained carbon dots could be real-time monitored in order to synthesize carbon dots with specific properties. By incorporating the carbon dots into a well-established enzymatic cascade amplification system, an inner filter effect-based fluorescence immunoassay was set up for ultrasensitive detection of cefquinome residues in milk samples. The developed fluorescence immunoassay provided a low detection limit of 0.78 ng/mL, which satisfied the maximum residue limit set by authorities. The fluorescence immunoassay had an 50% inhibition concentration of 0.19 ng/mL against cefquinome and showed a good linear relationship from 0.013 ng/mL to 1.52 ng/mL. While, the average recovery values ranged from 77.8% to 107.8% in spiked milk samples, with relative standard deviations ranging from 6.8% to 10.9%. Compared with conventional methods, the microfluidic chip was more flexible on carbon dots synthesis and the developed fluorescence immunoassay was more sensitive and eco-friendlier for ultra-trace cefquinome residue analysis.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Microfluidics
Fluorescence immunoassay
Cefquinome
Milk

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Carbon dots synthesis in the microfluidic chip
Artificial antigen synthesis and antibody production
Microfluidics device for carbon dots synthesis

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Setup of microfluidic chips to synthesized carbon dots at low temperature; (b) Channels of the microfluidic chips; (c) real-time monitoring carbon dots synthesis in microfluidic chips; (d) the fluorescence at the corners of the microfluidic chips.

The characterization of the synthesized carbon dots in microfluidic chips. (a) the TEM image, (b) the FTIR spectrum, (c) wide XPS spectrum of synthesized carbon dots; And high-resolution XPS survey scan spectrum of (d) C1s, (e) N1s, and (f) O1s.

(a) Fluorescence emission spectrum of the synthesized N-doped carbon dots at different excitation wavelengths with the inset photographs showing these N-doped carbon dots under natural light (left) and 365 nm UV light (right) illumination. (b) The absorption spectrum of oxABTS and the emission spectrum of the synthesized carbon dots under 365 nm UV light illumination.
Principle of the inner filter effect-based fluorescence immunoassay for detecting cefquinome residues in milk.
The calibration curves of conventional ELISA (a and b) and inner filter effect-based fluorescence immunoassay (c and d) for quantitative analysis of cefquinome residues in milk.
The recovery and coefficient of variation (CV) values of the developed fluorescence immunoassay for detecting cefquinome residues in raw milk and whole milk samples (n = 4).

head.ce:title: Low temperature synthesis of carbon dots in microfluidic chip and their application for sensing cefquinome residues in milk

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: Guangming Li and Chen Liu contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this study, the N-doped carbon dots were continuously synthesized by a facile microfluidic strategy at 90 °C, and their quantum yields reached 19.2%. The characteristics of the obtained carbon dots could be real-time monitored in order to synthesize carbon dots with specific properties. By incorporating the carbon dots into a well-established enzymatic cascade amplification system, an inner filter effect-based fluorescence immunoassay was set up for ultrasensitive detection of cefquinome residues in milk samples. The developed fluorescence immunoassay provided a low detection limit of 0.78 ng/mL, which satisfied the maximum residue limit set by authorities. The fluorescence immunoassay had an 50% inhibition concentration of 0.19 ng/mL against cefquinome and showed a good linear relationship from 0.013 ng/mL to 1.52 ng/mL. While, the average recovery values ranged from 77.8% to 107.8% in spiked milk samples, with relative standard deviations ranging from 6.8% to 10.9%. Compared with conventional methods, the microfluidic chip was more flexible on carbon dots synthesis and the developed fluorescence immunoassay was more sensitive and eco-friendlier for ultra-trace cefquinome residue analysis.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Since the discovery of penicillin and cephalosporins, the β-lactam antibiotics have been widely used for disease prevention and treatment by inhibiting the cell wall biosynthesis in bacteria (Sachi et al., 2019). Sharing a beta-lactam ring, the β-lactam antibiotics had good efficacy against the gram-positive bacteria (Zeng et al., 2013). However, some bacteria could gradually develop resistance to β-lactam antibiotics by synthesizing β-lactamases after extensive using in animal husbandry (Zhang et al., 2020). In order to reduce the occurrence of resistant bacteria, some newly developed atypical β-lactam antibiotics come into use in practice. As a fourth-generation β-lactam antibiotic, cefquinome (Fig. S1) showed good anti-microbial efficacy against most gram-positive bacteria and some gram-negative bacteria. Cefquinome had been extensively utilized for intramammary treatment of clinical and subclinical mastitis at dry-off in lactating dairy cows (Helmschrodt et al., 2018). However, the widely usage of cefquinome in animal husbandry was believed to be an important cause for the spread of antibiotics-resistant bacteria. The cefquinome residues also was proved to have relationship with allergy, enteric flora disturbance, and some other side effects (Zhang et al., 2013). To reduce health hazard from cefquinome contaminated food samples, the European Commission set maximum residue limit (MRL) to be 20 μg/kg in milk (Chen et al., 2016). While the Ministry of Agriculture of China has also set MRL values to be 20 μg/kg in milk and 200 μg/kg in kidney (Zhang et al., 2020).

Up to now, some analytical methods, such as, chromatography based instrument methods and enzyme or fluorescent tracers based immunoassays, had been reported for determination of cefquinome and other β-lactam antibiotics residues in different food matrices (Dorival-García et al., 2016; Zhang et al., 2020). However, the extensively utilized chromatographic methods are only useable on a laboratory equipped with professional technicians and expensive apparatus (Zhai et al., 2023). In most countries, enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay (ELISA) and other rapid-screening immunoassays were routinely performed for primary screening purposes (Yu et al., 2019, 2022). And the screened positive samples were further confirmed by chromatographic methods (Qu et al., 2019). Recently, several immunoassay formats were developed for the determination of penicillin and other β-lactam antibiotics (Zhang et al., 2013). But these reported immunoassays usually employed HRP as labels, and they suffered low sensitivity due to the low signal to noise ratio (Jiang et al., 2015). This made them unsuitable for detecting ultra-trace antibiotic residues in foods. In order to improve analytical performance, some researchers replaced absorption signal by fluorescence counterpart to achieve a higher signal to noise ratio (Dong et al., 2019; Xu et al., 2020). However, most reported fluorescence immunoassays sacrifice the enzymatic cascade signal amplification, and their sensitivity were only comparable with that of the ELISA (Lv et al., 2018; Qiu et al., 2017). Recently, we combined the enzymatic amplification and fluorescent signal readout, and developed an inner filter effect-based fluorescent immunoassay for chemical residue analysis (Li et al., 2021).

Carbon dots were a new carbon nanocrystal nanomaterial with diameter of less than 10 nm in size (Lin et al., 2017; Qu et al., 2018; Yu et al., 2020). As an emerging non-toxic and eco-friendly carbon nanomaterial, carbon dots have been applied for ultrasensitive and feasible analytical method development for various antibiotic residue analysis (Fu et al., 2020). Due to their high quantum yield, excitation-dependent photoluminescence, good chemical stability and biocompatibility, carbon dots have been extensively applied in biosensing, bioimaging, nanomedicine and many other research fields (Zhao et al., 2021). Recently, novel inner filter effect-based fluorescent ELISA methods had been reported by introducing carbon dots into ELISA system for detecting various analytes residue analysis via the inner filter effect to obtain a higher sensitivity (Dong et al., 2019; Xu et al., 2020). During the last one decade, a wide variety of approaches had been reported for synthesizing the eco-friendly carbon dots (de Medeiros et al., 2019; Jing et al., 2019; Liu et al., 2017; Sharma and Das, 2019). However, these conventional synthesize methods usually resulted in low reproducibility and large size distribution. Besides, all the carbon dots synthesized by the above methods has the disadvantage of unchangeable fluorescence properties. In order to establish inner filter effect-based fluorescence immunoassays, we need to synthesize carbon dots with specific fluorescence properties, highly monodispersed and reproducible form.

Microfluidics were defined as the science and technology of systems, and they can manipulate ultra-trace amount of fluids (10−9 to 10−18 L) in the micro-channels measuring from tens to hundreds of micrometers (Escarpa, 2014). The microfluidic device allows mass transfer and abrupt heat, resulting in rapid heating and cooling of reagents that maintains an isothermal condition (Illath et al., 2022). They can real-time monitor the reaction processes in the temporal and spatial domains, and provide flexible reactions in micro-channels (Elvira et al., 2013). Since 2006, the miniaturized microfluidics had gained much more popularity due to their attractive inherent features, i.e., automation, fast analysis, and low reagent consumption (Whitesides, 2006). Hence, new microfluidic devices were frequently applicated in disease diagnosis and environmental analysis (Liu et al., 2020; Nasseri et al., 2018). However, few efforts were made on carbon dots synthesis in the microfluidic chips.

In this study, we synthesize N-doped carbon dots at low temperature in visual micro-channels of self-designed microfluidics device. The excitation/emission spectrum of the N-doped carbon dots could be overlapped by the absorption spectrum of the oxidate of the ELISA substrate, 2′-azino-bis(3-ethylbenzothiazoline-6-sulfonic acid) (ABTS), and the efficient inner filter effect could occur between absorbers and fluorophores. Hence, we combined the synthesized carbon dots with cascade enzymatic amplification via the inner filter effect to establish a label free immunoassay for cefquinome residue analysis. The new microplate-based fluorescence immunoassay prescribed in this paper was a promising and reliable on-site screening method for cefquinome residue analysis.

Cefquinome and structural related standards were obtained from Ark Pharm, Inc. (Libertyville, IL, US). Diethylenetriamine, ascorbic acid, horseradish peroxidase (HRP) labelled goat anti-mouse IgG, bovine serum albumin (BSA), and ovalbumin (OVA) were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich (St. Louis, MO, US). One-step ABTS substrate solution was purchased from Thermo Fisher Scientific (Waltham, MA, US). The PDMS Sylgard 184 consisting of base and curing agent was obtained from Dow Corning Corporation (Midland, MI, US). Transparent and white opaque 96-well microtiter plates were purchased from Costar Inc. (Milpitas, CA, US). The absorbance and fluorescence intensity were recorded by a Tecan Infinite 200 PRO microplate reader (Männedorf, Switzerland).

In this paper, we fabricated three-dimensional microfluidic chips in poly(dimethylsiloxane) (PDMS) using soft lithography (Liu et al., 2020). Briefly, the negative SU-8 3050 photoresist was spun onto a 3-inch silicon wafer to form a 100 μm thick negative photoresist layer through a spin-coating procedure. The wafer was baked at 95 °C for 15 min, and then it was covered with a photomask for exposure under ultraviolet (UV) light by a mask aligner. After removing the mask and baking the wafer again at 95 °C, the uncured resist was dissolved with propylene glycol monomethyl ether acetate to obtain the SU-8 master mold with specific microchannels. The master mold was baked at 95 °C for 5 min, and the PDMS base prepolymer and curing agent mixture was poured on the master mold in order to replicate specific microchannels form the master mold. After degassing and curing at 65 °C for 60 min, the PDMS with specific microchannels was peeled from the master mold, and it was bonded with a glass microscope slide via plasma treatments after making through-holes as inlets or outlets. Finally, the fabricated microfluidic chips were baking at 70 °C for 6 h in order to enhance the bonding strength.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Carbon dots synthesis in the microfluidic chip
ce:section.ce:para: The N-doped carbon dots were continually synthesized in the prepared microfluidic chip at low temperature. Briefly, 0.25 g ascorbic acid dissolved in 5.0 ml mixed solvent (deionized water/ethylene glycol = 1:1 in volume ratio) solution was injected into inlet 1 by syringe pump, and 0.25 g diethylenetriamine dissolved in 5.0 ml mixed solvent (deionized water/ethylene glycol = 1:1 in volume ratio) was injected into inlet 2 of the microfluidic chips. Then, the two solutions were mixed in the spiral channels of the prepared microfluidic chips, and the microfluidic chips were put into a thermostatic oil bath at 95 °C. The flow rate of two solutions were set at 20 μL/h. The reaction mixture solution was collected form the outlet of the microfluidic chip. Finally, the synthesized N-doped carbon dots were purified by dialysis (500 Da, MWCO) for 24 h.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Artificial antigen synthesis and antibody production
ce:section.ce:para: Cefquinome was conjugated with carrier proteins according to our previous report with the following modifications (He et al., 2017). Briefly, 12 mg of cefquinome, 11 mg of 1-ethyl-3-(3-dimethylaminopropyl)carbodiimide hydrochloride and 5.5 mg of N-hydroxysuccinimide were dissolved in 2000 μL of N, N-dimethylformamide. Then, 8 mg of BSA or 5 mg OVA were dissolved in 4 mL of borate buffer. Afterwards, the activated cefquinome solutions were dropwise added to the carrier protein solutions, and the mixture reacted at 4 °C for another 12 h. The synthesized artificial antigens were dialyzed against distilled water for 72 h, and then the supernatant was collected after centrifugation at 3000×g for 10 min. The monoclonal antibody was generated according our previous report (Liu et al., 2021), and the purified antibody was preserved at −20 °C for posterior immunoassay development.

The cefquinome-OVA was used as coating antigen and diluted with carbonate buffer (0.05 M, pH 9.6), and 100 μL/well of the diluted coating antigen was added to the microplates. After incubating overnight at 4 °C, the plates were blocked using 0.01 M PBS containing 1% casein for 60 min at 4 °C. After discarding the block solution, the plates were washing by 0.02M PBS containing 0.5% Tween-20. Then, 50 μL/well of the cefquinome solutions and 50 μL/well of the prepared anti-cefquinome antibody solutions were pipetted to each well, and the plates were incubated at 25 °C for 30 min. After another washing process, 100 μL of ABTS substrates were added, and the plated were incubated again at 25 °C for 20 min. Finally, the absorbance was recorded at 420 nm for ELISA development. While, 10 μL of diluted carbon dots solutions were added, and the fluorescence intensity of each well was recorded for fluorescence immunoassay development.

The cefquinome standard solutions were pipetted to the microplates in triplicate wells, and their average fluorescence intensity values were plotted against the cefquinome concentration. After fitting the calibration curves by sigmoidal function, the 50% inhibitory concentration (IC50) value was calculated to evaluate the sensitivity of the new established fluorescence immunoassay (Li et al., 2021). To evaluate the selectivity, other structurally related cross-reactants were tested by the fluorescence immunoassay. The cross-reactivity of other structural-related analytes was evaluated by the following equation: Cross-reactivity (%) = (IC50 of cefquinome)/(IC50 of other analytes) × 100%

The purchased raw milk and whole milk samples were verified to be free of cefquinome and other β-lactam antibiotics contamination by a well-established LC-MS/MS (General Administration of Quality Supervision, 2008). The milk samples were defatted by centrifugation at 10, 000×g for 10 min at 4 °C. Then, the defatted milk was extracted by 0.05M PBS (pH 3.5) containing 83% acetonitrile, and diluted 10 times with assay buffer prior to analysis. The proposed fluorescence immunoassay was further validated by evaluating its accuracy and precision using the verified milk samples.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Microfluidics device for carbon dots synthesis
ce:section.ce:para: In general, the carbon dots synthesize method usually utilized individual molecules to synthesize carbon dots using liquid state (or wet chemical). However, these processes usually require toxic solvent environment (strong acid), high energy input and high temperature (160–200 °C). Consequently, reproducible synthesis of carbon dots at low temperature is still a crucial problem for scalable production and practical application. Particularly, it was still a challenge to precisely synthesize carbon dots with desired characteristics, and real-time monitoring the characteristics of the synthesized carbon dots was not available.

Microfluidic device was being embraced by scientists as a powerful tool to perform precise experiments, and they have become popular research and development topics in the field of medical and life science. To enhance the performance, we setup a new microfluidic device to continuously synthesize N-doped carbon dots at low temperature (Fig. 1 a). Since the introduction of microfluidic devices in the early 1990s, silicon and glass had been the dominant substrate material for fabricating lab on chips (Harrison et al., 1993). Recently, PDMS, poly(methylmethacrylate) and other polymer materials have been reported for microfluidic chips fabrication. In this study, we fabricated a PDMS-based microfluidic chip using Si mold and mask making done at Harvard (Fig. 1b). As depicted in Fig. 1b and Fig. S2a, the microfluidic chip included two inlets (reagent injecting zone), a micro-mixing zone, subsequent reaction microchannels, and an outlet (product collecting zone). To synthesize carbon dots, the reagents were injected to the microfluidic chip heated at 90 °C in oil bath. The inlet pumps the reagents into the microfluidic chip, and the reagents were mixed in the micro-mixing zone. Then, sufficient residence time were provided in the long micro-channels of the chip, and the synthesized carbon dots can be collected from the outlet (Fig. 1c). Recently, the zig-zag, intersecting, and 3D serpentine geometry structures had been reported to mix multiple miscible solvents (Illath et al., 2022). As shown in Fig. S2 and the Supplementary Video 1, the 3D serpentine structure achieved satisfying mixing efficiency carbon dots synthesis in the microfluidic platform.

In this study, we continually synthesized carbon dots in microfluidic chip at low temperature. The synthesized carbon dots solutions were collected from the outlet of the microfluidic chip, and then we can directly characterize the quantum yields, emission spectrum and other performance of the synthesized carbon dots. If the characteristics of the collected carbon dots cannot satisfy the requirements of immunoassay development, we can change the reagents concentration, the flow rate, reaction temperature and other reaction parameters until we synthesized satisfying carbon dots. In contrast to one-pot strategy, the characteristics of the carbon dots synthesized in the PDMS-based microfluidic chip could be real-time monitored.

In Table S1, we reviewed recent advances on carbon dots synthesis. In order to obtain carbon dots with excellent performance, we optimized reaction time, molar ratio, and reaction temperature. At the optimal reaction time (0.5 h), molar ratio (1:1), and reaction temperature (90 °C), the carbon dots were successfully synthesized in the micro-channels of microfluidic chips. The obtained carbon dots in the micro-channels were yellow transparent liquid in visible light (Fig. 1c). The results in Figs. S2b and S2c indicated that the microfluidic chip was still stable after reaction up to 7 days. The microfluidic chip-based strategy was more flexible, and the reaction conditions could be instantly optimized according to the quality of the reaction products.

In traditional reports, one pot strategy had been widely utilized to synthesize carbon dots by heat starting materials in a flask or similar reactor. To the best of our knowledge, it was the first time that microfluidic chips were utilized as a reactor to synthesize N-doped carbon dots at low temperature. In this study, we utilized the microfluidic chip as reactor to synthesize carbon dots by heating the whole chip in the oil bath. When the two starting materials were pumped into the microfluidic chip, they were gently mixed in the mixer region of the chip, and then they were heating on the long reaction channels. When the two starting materials were pumped from inlets to the outlet, the carbon dots could be graduate synthesized in the microfluidic chip on moderate temperature. Finally, the synthesized carbon dots were collected form the outlet of the microfluidic chip for posterior experiments. The key benefit of the microfluidic chip fabricated in this study is precise control over the volume of the reagents and continuous synthesis of carbon dots, allowing real-time monitoring of characteristics of the synthesized carbon dots. Moreover, the tunable chip design and reagents flow rate could provide flexible reaction parameters for carbon dots synthesis. Thus, the microfluidic chip had shown promises for synthesizing carbon dots with specific characteristics and high reproducibility, which offers a research pilot platform for carbon dots and other nanomaterials synthesis.

The transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images of the synthesized carbon dots were taken with FEI Tecnai G2 S-Twin TEM (Hillsboro, OR, USA) operated at 200 kV. The obtained carbon dots are spherical and have uniform particle size of around 4 nm with good dispersion (Fig. 2 a). The fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectrum of the obtained carbon dots were recorded using a standard KBr pellet technique on a Vertex 70 FTIR spectrometer (Karlsruhe, Germany). As shown in Fig. 2b, the stretching vibrations at 3080–3658 cm−1 can be attributed to O–H and N–H, while the peaks at 1642 cm−1 and 1588 cm−1 are associated with vibrational absorption band of CO and the bending vibrations of N–H. FTIR results show that the surface of carbon dots contains many hydroxyl, carboxyl and amino groups.

The X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was acquired on an ESCALlab220i-XL electron spectrometer (Waltham, MA, USA) using 300ALKα radiations to explore its chemical bonds. As shown in Fig. 2c, full-scan XPS spectrum indicates that the as-synthesized carbon dots are mainly composed of C (285.4 eV), N (398.6 eV), and O (530.2 eV) elements. In Fig. 2d, three peaks in centered at 284.6, 285.7, and 287.7 eV suggests the presence of CC, C–O, and C–N, respectively. In Fig. 2e, the binding energy peaks at 399.6 eV and 401.0 eV can be attributed to (N-(C)3) and (N–H). While in Fig. 2f, the two peaks at 531.6 and 533.0 eV in the O1s spectrum can be assigned to C–OH and C–N–C, respectively.

The quantum yield of the N-doped carbon dots was evaluated using a C-9920-02 absolute PL quantum yield spectrometer (Hamamatsu, Japan), and it reached to 19.2%, which was superior to most reports. Further, we also investigated the fluorescence spectrum of synthesized carbon dots in order to evaluate their fluorescent properties. As shown in Fig. 3 a, the fluorescence spectrum of the synthesized carbon dots shows the redshift in the range of 350–700 nm, and their emission intensity gradually decreased with the excitation wavelength increasing from 350 nm to 410 nm. In the inset of Fig. 3a, the synthesized carbon dots were slight yellow aqueous solutions, and they can emit blue photoluminescence under the irrigation of UV light.

When the synthesized carbon dots were excited at 350 nm, its emission fluorescence intensity were relative stable up to 20 min (Fig. S3a). Also, we evaluated the fluorescence lifetime of the synthesized carbon dots and carbon dots + quenching agent. As shown in Fig. S3b, the fluorescence lifetime of the carbon dots + quenching agent had almost no difference with that of the carbon dots. The addition of analyte would not interfere the fluorescence lifetime of the synthesized carbon dots. These results proved that the quenching mechanism of the established fluorescence immunoassay was determined to be the inner filter effect between the oxABTS from the ELISA system and the synthesized carbon dots.

Some previous reports found that carbon dots and some other nanomaterials may have a kind of nanozyme self-catalysis characteristic. To investigate fluorescence quenching mechanism of the reaction system, we evaluated the fluorescence intensity of the carbon dots + assay buffer, carbon dots + ABTS, carbon dots + H2O2, carbon dots + HRP, carbon dots + ABTS + H2O2, carbon dots + HRP + ABTS + H2O2. As shown in Fig. S4, the presence of ABTS + H2O2 + HRP could lead to the fluorescence quenching. While, the fluorescence quenching phenomenon was not caused by ABTS + H2O2 + carbon dots, which proved that the carbon dots have nanozyme self-catalysis propriety. The results in the following figure also proved that other components in the ELISA system will not affect the quenching.

In order to improve analytical performance, we incorporated the synthesized carbon dots into a well-established ELISA system to develop an inner filter effect-based fluorescence immunoassay (Fig. 4 ). The ABTS (chromogenic substrate of HRP) and their oxide products (oxABTS) were tested in the synthesized carbon dots solutions to evaluate their fluorescence quenching efficiency. The maximum UV–vis absorbance spectrum of ABTS was at 375 nm, and it had neglectable effects on evaluating the carbon dots’ fluorescence intensity at 425 nm (Fig. S5). While, the maximum absorption spectrum of oxABTS (420 nm) could be overlapped by the maximum emission wavelength of the synthesized carbon dots (425 nm), and the inner filter effect could occur in the detection system (Fig. 3b). After adding carbon dots to the ELISA catalytic systems, the HRP triggered fluorescent signal transition and amplification between the carbon dots and the oxidate of the ABTS substrates using H2O2 as an oxidant. Thus, the assay sensitivity of the fluorescence immunoassay could be improved by transmitted the absorbance of ELISA into fluorescence intensity. The new established fluorescence immunoassays were easy to operate (no need to link absorbers with fluorophores), and they are much more sensitivity than conventional ELISA.

The enzymatic process needs a stable environment with specific pH and ingredient concentrations. Unsuitable pH and ingredient concentrations would result low absorbance and assay sensitivity. Thus, we added a detailed optimization of the fluorescence immunoassay according to previous reports (Xu et al., 2020). From the results of Fig. S6, we can conclude that the developed immunoassay needs a pH value at 4. As shown in Fig. S7 and Fig. S8, we can conclude that the developed immunoassay needs a ABTS concentration at 80 mM and a H2O2 concentration at 8–10 mM.

In this paper, a checkerboard titration was utilized to evaluate the optimal immunoreagents concentrations required to establish an ELISA method for cefquinome residue analysis (Jiang et al., 2015). Then, the immunoassay data were fitted by a dynamic four-parameter equation, and a calibration curve was established in Fig. 5 a. The standard curve of the established ELISA showed a good linear relationship (R = 0.962) in the range of 0.22–21.8 ng/mL, and the IC50 value was estimated to be 1.68 ng/mL for cefquinome (Fig. 5b). For most reported ELISA and other immunoassays, the milk samples usually need to be diluted 10 or more times for screening purpose. Thus, it was still a great challenge to determine ultra-trace cefquinome residues in dairy products for orphans or people allergic to any β-lactam antibiotics.

Then, a series of cefquinome standard solutions was prepared in assay buffer, and the calibration curves of the fluorescence immunoassay were built by plotting the fluorescence intensity against the cefquinome concentrations on a logarithmic scale. As shown in Fig. 5c, the recorded fluorescence intensity values gradually increased with the cefquinome concentration increasing from 0.01 ng/mL to 2.7 ng/mL. And the fluorescence intensity values saturated when the cefquinome concentration reached 2.7 ng/mL. Besides, the obtained calibration curves had a satisfying linear relationship (R = 0.986) with cefquinome concentration in the range of 0.013–1.52 ng/mL (Fig. 5d). These results confirmed that the fluorescence quenching was triggered by the inner filter effect between the synthesized carbon dots and the HRP-ABTS-H2O2 catalytic system. The fluorescence intensity vs cefquinome concentration values were fitted by above-mentioned four-parameter equations, and the IC50 value was estimated to be 0.19 ng/mL.

Further, we evaluated the selectivity of the fluorescence immunoassay by determining the cross-reactivity with other β-lactam antibiotics. In the cross-reactivity studies, the 0.1 ng/mL of cefquinome solution could remarkably increase the fluorescence intensity values, whereas 10 ng/mL of other β-lactam antibiotic solutions did not cause obvious fluorescence changes over the control wells. As summarized in Table S2, the negligible cross-reactivity with other β-lactam antibiotic indicated that co-existence of the tested β-lactam antibiotics would not interfere with cefquinome residue analysis.

As a complex emulsion matrix, the milk contains proteins, lipids, sugar, minerals, and other nutrients. The stability of cefquinome in milk matrix is extremely important for rapid screening methods to estimate its residues. Previous studies indicated that the β-lactam group of the cefquinome could be degraded by temperature, photo-dissociation, the pH and the neutral salts concentration of the solutions, as well as air humidity (Helmschrodt et al., 2018). In this paper, we evaluated the stability of the cefquinome in the spiked milk and in the milk extracts at 50 ng/mL at varying temperatures. The results in Fig. S9 indicated that the storage of the spiked milk samples at 25 °C and 4 °C had >25% losses after 7 days. While, the storage of spiked milk samples at −20 °C had slow degradation (<10%). In contrast, the cefquinome in the milk extracts showed relatively good stability, and the cefquinome concentrations only have slow degradation (3.9%) at −20 °C. This results were to some extent in accordance with previous studies (Riediker et al., 2004).

In order to remove the food matrix interferences, the sample pretreatment process is extremely important for the newly established immunoassays. For investigating the applicability in facing up real milk matrices, the matrix spiked calibration curves in the diluted milk, and extracted milk were tested by proposed fluorescence immunoassay. The results in Fig. S10 indicated the matrix spiked cefquinome standard curve in milk extract agree well with the calibration curve in assay buffer. Thus, the defatted milk samples should be extracted by PBS containing acetonitrile, and then diluted ten times by assay buffer for posterior analysis.

In most cases, the complicated milk ingredients could cause false-positive or false-negative results by lowering color development in conventional ELISAs or quenching fluorescent signals in fluorescence immunoassays. In the new established fluorescence immunoassay, we directly added N-doped carbon dots to an HRP-based ELISA system after the colorimetric reaction. Under this condition, the added carbon dots would not be quenched by the milk matrices, and its analytical performance would be enhanced in real food sample analysis. Thus, the established fluorescence immunoassay was more suitable for screening the cefquinome residues in milk.

In this paper, the established fluorescence immunoassay was validated according to the regulation from Eurachem Guide and our previous report (Li et al., 2021). According to the average values plus 3 times of the calculated standard deviations of the verified blank samples, the limit of detection (LOD) was 0.78 ng/mL, which has satisfied the MRLs of cefquinome in milk by European Union and People's Republic of China.

The accuracy and reproducibility of the fluorescence immunoassay was proven by analyzing artificially spiked samples. The verified blank milk samples were spiked at three cefquinome concentration levels (low, middle, high). The milk extracts were tested by the established fluorescence immunoassay, and no non-spiked samples were screened as positive results. Furthermore, the detected cefquinome concentration from spiked milk samples showed relatively good agreements with that of the spiked milk samples in the range of 1 ng/mL to 10 ng/mL. As shown in Table 1 , the developed fluorescence immunoassay obtained the mean recovery values in the range of 77.8%–107.8%, with intra- and inter-assay coefficient of variation (CV) values in the range of 6.8%–10.9%.

For the final validation, the real application of the fluorescence immunoassay was further confirmed by a well-established LC-MS/MS method by detecting naturally contaminated samples. The cefquinome concentrations were determined to be 7.2 ng/mL and 10.2 ng/mL by LC-MS/MS according to the procedure of GB/T22989-2008. The concentrations were determined to be 0.77 ng/mL and 1.13 ng/mL by the developed fluorescence immunoassay, while its dilution factor was 0.1 for milk samples. Thus, the corrected concentrations were 7.7 ng/mL and 11.3 ng/mL for the developed fluorescence immunoassay. These results proved that the developed fluorescence immunoassay has a good agreement with the LC-MS/MS method.

Recently, the antibody or penicillin binding protein-based immunoassays, especially the ELISA and the lateral flow strip, are powerful tools on screening β-lactam antibiotic and other chemical residues in foods due to its higher throughput (He et al., 2017). Since 2003, some researchers generate new anti-β-lactam antibiotics antibody or penicillin binding proteins, and based on which, new antibody- or receptor-based immunoassays had been developed for the determination of β-lactam antibiotic residues before confirmation by chromatography methods (Thal et al., 2011; Zeng et al., 2013; Zhang et al., 2013; Li et al., 2020). In Table S3, we summarized recent advances of analytical methods for on-site screening cefquinome and other β-lactam antibiotic residues in milk and other food matrices. The new generated antibody or receptor usually had IC50 values in the range of 1.1–40.5 ng/mL, thus, resulted in a higher detection limit (>4 μg/L) in milk or other food matrices. Although these reported immunoassays were easy-to-operate, their sensitivities were insufficient to reach the stringent detection limits for on-site detecting cefquinome or other β-lactam antibiotic residues in milk and other food matrices. Thus, we introduced the inner filter effect into the fluorescence immunoassay to improve its analytical performance.

During the last one decade, some new nanomaterials have been extensively synthesized and utilized for food safety surveillance purpose. In this study, we continuously synthesized N-doped carbon dots in the micro-channels of the self-designed microfluidic chips, and they have a high quantum yield of 19.2%. The evaluated characteristics of the synthesized carbon dots were superior to most reports in Table S2. In order to establish an inner filter effect-based fluorescence immunoassay, we should synthesize carbon dots with specific excitation/emission spectrum in order to overlap the absorbance spectrum of enzymatic substrate in the ELISA system. In contrast to one pot strategy, we utilized a self-designed microfluidic reactor to precisely synthesize carbon dots at low temperature. After collecting from the outlet of the microfluidic chip, and immediate feedback from the characteristics of the synthesized carbon dots were evaluated in order to precisely control reaction parameters to synthesize desirable carbon dots with specific excitation/emission spectrum. Compared with other nanomaterial probes, the obtained carbon dots have tunable fluorescence emission, and they are desirable for the fabrication of new fluorescence immunoassays. After introducing the desirable carbon dots into a well-established cefquinome ELISA, a novel inner filter effect-based fluorescence immunoassay would be fabricated by triggering the fluorescence signal transition and amplification between the carbon dots with specific excitation/emission spectrum and the oxABTS with absorbance spectrum. Moreover, the carbon dots were directly added to the enzymatic substrate of the ELISA system to establish a label-free fluorescence immunoassay, and the generated fluorescent signals would not be quenched by some food ingredients from milk matrix. This characteristic made the synthesized carbon dots suitable for fabricating label-free fluorescence immunoassays for estimating the ultra-trace cefquinome residues in milk, and the assay sensitivity could be directly improved about 4–10 times. In contrast to other reports, the label-free fluorescence immunoassays could be a reliable tool for sensitively and selectively sensing cefquinome residues.

In this paper, we setup a new microfluidic chip to synthesize N-doped carbon dots at low temperature for the first time. The synthesized carbon dots were collected from the outlet of the microfluidic chip, and the characteristics of the carbon dots synthesized by the microfluidic strategy could be real-time monitored. Then, the reaction parameters would be optimized accordingly in order to synthesize carbon dots with specific properties. The carbon dots were continuously prepared in the microchannels of the microfluidic chip, and their quantum yields reached 19.2%. We incorporated the obtained carbon dots into conventional colorimetric ELISA systems, and an inner filter effect-based fluorescence immunoassay was established for sensing ultra-trace cefquinome residues in milk. The developed fluorescence immunoassay obtained a lower LOD of 0.78 ng/mL, and its analytical performance was superior to conventional ELISA. The label free fluorescence immunoassay should be a more flexible and promising strategy for high-throughput screening cefquinome residues in milk.

Guangming Li: Experimental design and, Formal analysis. Chen Liu: Project administration, Writing – original draft, manuscript. Xingcai Zhang: Conceptualization. Peng Zhai: Validation. Xinyi Lai: Project administration. Wenxiao Jiang: Supervision, Funding acquisition, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 31972763 and No. 31602103), Natural Science Foundation of Guangdong Province, China (No. 2023A1515012152, No. 2022A1515110369 and No. 2019A1515011834), Shenzhen Basic Research Project (No. JCYJ20190808160003705), and Shenzhen Peacock Talent Program (No. 827/000653).
